source.title: Does Working From Home Work? Evidence from a Chinese Experiment
source.cite: Bloom, Liang, Roberts & Ying · Stanford / Ctrip · 2015

annotations.quote: Home working led to a 13% improvement in performance
annotations.note: Headline experimental result: direct positive evidence on the root question.

annotations.quote: four from handling more calls per minute in a quieter, more convenient environment
annotations.note: The per-call gain is on solo, focused call-handling — evidence for the solo-tasks node.

annotations.quote: Yet when later given the choice, over half asked to return to the office — and the gains for that group narrowed once they did.
annotations.note: Gains partially reversed later — the short- vs long-term distinction matters.

annotations.quote: The setting is a specific one: routine, individually-measured call-centre work, where output is unusually easy to quantify.
annotations.note: Call-centre staff only — bounds which workers the result covers.

annotations.quote: The setting is a specific one: routine, individually-measured call-centre work, where output is unusually easy to quantify.
annotations.note: Routine, easily-measured tasks — may not generalise to collaborative or creative work.

annotations.quote: Home workers reported higher satisfaction, and attrition fell by half.
annotations.note: Secondary positive outcomes adjacent to productivity; weak-but-relevant support for the root.
